critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a well-structured experiment design. I'll work through each potential issue systematically, grouping by root cause.

---

### Group 1: Temporal Leakage and the Stratified Split Design

**Claim:** Stratification by depot, season, and class prevalence across train/val/test prevents confounding and ensures fair evaluation.

**Why this might be wrong:** The design stratifies at the route-day level but does not enforce a temporal ordering of the splits. If train, validation, and test data are interleaved in time (i.e., a route from Month 30 could appear in training while a route from Month 2 appears in test), then features that are implicitly time-correlated — driver tenure, vehicle capacity utilization, depot queue depth — effectively encode future information into models trained on nominally 'earlier' data. More critically, operational regimes change: a depot that introduces a new scanning protocol in Month 20 would have its post-protocol routes distributed across all three folds, making it impossible to assess whether the model would generalize to a new operational regime it has never seen.

The standard defense in time-series operational data is a strict temporal cutoff split, not stratified random sampling. Stratified sampling is appropriate for i.i.d. data; operational delivery logs are explicitly not i.i.d. due to autocorrelation in driver behavior, depot throughput patterns, and weather episodes.

**Evidence:** Compare model performance when the split is done by strict temporal cutoff (train = months 1–24, val = months 25–30, test = months 31–36) versus the described stratified approach. A significant AUC drop under temporal cutoff would confirm that the stratified design is leaking temporal signal.

---

### Group 2: Label Definition Creates Asymmetric Signal Availability

**Claim:** The label — 'any package on a route missed its window by >15 minutes as confirmed by final scan timestamp' — is consistent and usable as a training target.

**Why this might be wrong — two sub-issues:**

(a) **Label availability at prediction time vs. training time.** The experiment does not state *when* during the route the prediction is made. If the model is intended as a pre-departure or mid-route warning system, then scan-derived features like 'time-since-last-scan,' 'cumulative scans in the last 4 hours,' and 'scan-to-scan intervals' may not all be available at prediction time. But the label itself is only known at route completion. If the model is evaluated on features from the full route trajectory (as logged historically), it will learn from data that partially post-dates the decision point, creating look-ahead leakage in the features even if not in the label.

(b) **The 'any package' threshold is a noisy aggregate.** A route with 120 packages where one package was delayed 16 minutes due to a customer not answering the door is labeled the same as a route where 80 packages were delayed by 2 hours due to a vehicle breakdown. The label conflates route-level operational failure with individual package exceptions. If individual package exceptions (access issues, customer absence) are not systematically predictable from route-level features, they add label noise that suppresses true AUC and inflates apparent model uncertainty.

**Evidence for (a):** Document the exact prediction point in the operational workflow. Audit features to confirm none are derived from post-prediction-point scans. If prediction is at route departure, features like 'cumulative scans in last 4 hours' may be fine; if prediction is at depot assignment (earlier), many scan features are not yet realized.

**Evidence for (b):** Compute the distribution of 'number of late packages per late route.' If a large fraction of positive labels correspond to routes with exactly 1 late package, examine whether those single-package delays correlate with any route-level feature. Low correlation would confirm label noise from individual exceptions.

---

### Group 3: The Baseline Comparison Does Not Isolate the Right Variable

**Claim:** Comparing XGBoost with route-level features against logistic regression with aggregated features isolates the value of 'route-level granularity and event sequences.'

**Why this might be wrong:** This comparison conflates two variables: model class (gradient boosting vs. logistic regression) and feature granularity (route-level vs. depot-day aggregates). If XGBoost outperforms logistic regression, the experiment cannot distinguish whether the gain comes from (a) nonlinear interactions in route-level features, (b) the route-level features themselves regardless of model class, or (c) the superior capacity of gradient boosting to exploit any feature set.

A proper isolation requires at minimum a third condition: logistic regression trained on the same route-level features as XGBoost. If logistic regression with route-level features already captures most of the AUC gain, that suggests the features are the differentiating factor, not the model. If XGBoost with aggregated features also outperforms logistic regression with aggregated features substantially, that suggests model class is doing significant work.

**Evidence:** Add logistic regression (tuned with same effort) on the full route-level feature set as a third comparison arm. The pattern of results across all four cells (2 models × 2 feature sets) disambiguates the source of any performance gain.

---

### Group 4: Held-Out Depot Evaluation Has an Underspecified Failure Criterion

**Claim:** The held-out depot serves as a 'diagnostic check for generalization.' Poor performance signals depot-specific overfitting.

**Why this might be wrong:** The design does not specify what constitutes 'poor performance' on the held-out depot relative to the test-set AUC, nor does it control for the possibility that the held-out depot is genuinely harder or easier than the training depots for reasons unrelated to overfitting — e.g., it handles a different package mix, operates in a different climate zone, or uses different scanning equipment. A 10-point AUC drop on the held-out depot could mean overfitting, or it could mean the held-out depot is operationally dissimilar in ways that no model trained on other depots could be expected to generalize to.

Without a depot-difficulty baseline (e.g., a simple majority-class or depot-historical-average-rate model evaluated on the held-out depot), there is no way to determine whether the absolute gap is an artifact of depot difficulty or a failure of generalization.

**Evidence:** Evaluate the baseline logistic regression model on the held-out depot under the same conditions. If the XGBoost-to-baseline gap on the held-out depot mirrors the gap on the in-distribution test set, that is evidence that the model generalizes; if the gap collapses on the held-out depot, that specifically implicates the route-level features as non-generalizing.

---

### Group 5: ROC-AUC as Primary Metric for an Operational Intervention System

**Claim:** ROC-AUC 'provides a prevalence-stable view' and 'aligns with the core experimental question.'

**Why this might be wrong:** AUC is prevalence-stable in the theoretical sense but can be misleading in practice when class prevalence varies across depots. At 5% prevalence, a model that correctly orders 95% of the positives can still have a high AUC while producing extremely poor precision at any operationally useful threshold. More importantly, the design uses AUC for model selection but then applies a separate threshold optimized on F1 or target precision on validation data — this is correct procedure, but it means the operational metric (F1 or precision at threshold) is not driving model selection. It is possible to select a model with higher AUC that achieves lower precision-recall at the operational threshold than a model with slightly lower AUC but better-calibrated probability outputs.

This is not a fatal flaw but is a meaningful tension. The design's secondary metrics partially address it, but model selection should arguably be on the metric closest to operational use.

**Evidence:** Report the Precision-Recall AUC (average precision) alongside ROC-AUC. At 5–15% prevalence, average precision is more sensitive to model behavior in the operationally relevant regime. If model rankings differ between ROC-AUC and average precision, that is actionable.

---

### Summary Assessment

The most significant issue is Group 1 (temporal leakage via stratified random split). Operational delivery data has strong temporal autocorrelation, and a stratified random split across 36 months is likely to create information leakage that inflates AUC on both validation and test. This is a root-cause flaw that would affect all downstream conclusions.

Group 3 (confounded baseline) is a genuine secondary flaw: the design as written cannot attribute performance gains to features vs. model class, which is its stated experimental question.

Groups 2, 4, and 5 are real design gaps but are more amenable to post-hoc analysis or minor design additions rather than fundamental reconstruction.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across 36 months of operational data likely introduces temporal leakage because operationally autocorrelated features (driver tenure, depot queue depth, scan patterns) from later time periods are distributed into training, violating the temporal independence assumption required for valid generalization claims.

The baseline comparison conflates model class and feature granularity — XGBoost with route-level features vs. logistic regression with aggregated features cannot isolate whether gains come from feature richness, nonlinear interactions, or model capacity, because a logistic regression on route-level features is absent.

Label definition ('any package >15 min late') conflates route-level operational failures with individual package exceptions (customer absence, access issues) that may be unpredictable from route-level features, introducing label noise that suppresses true signal and distorts AUC.
Scan-derived features used as predictors may post-date the intended prediction point in the operational workflow, creating look-ahead feature leakage even when the label itself is correctly handled.
The held-out depot evaluation lacks a baseline difficulty reference, making it impossible to distinguish model overfitting from the held-out depot being operationally harder by construction.

Model selection on ROC-AUC while applying an operationally-selected threshold creates a mismatch: the selected model may not be optimal under precision-recall criteria at the deployment threshold, and average precision is absent as a comparison metric at low prevalence.
